Previously, xpconanfan (Ang Li) wrote…
does `log.error` log the stacktrace? should this be `log.exception`?
The implementation of original snippet_client uses log.exception, but I found it is a problem of python2, not python3. According to internal post "Python tips 046", re-raising in python3 does preserve trace. I have did experiments to verify the conclusion. So if we use `log.exception` here, we will print the error trace twice. Also, I found in experiments that the error trace printed by log.exception is incomplete when the error occurs in a context manager. While the trace printed by re-raising is complete. So in my opinion the following code is the best practice of re-raising, does that make sense? ``` except Exception as e: log.error(....) raise ``` ___ *[mobly/snippet/client_base.py, line 235 at r1](https://reviewable.io/reviews/google/mobly/795#-Mwd_58k9qDiSxg8WorX:-MwfcXTdBZQ1poqfIXTr:b-t0s805) ([raw file](https://github.com/google/mobly/blob/6140b5a29d4e6c4f4d666e9c38b716fed01d058c/mobly/snippet/client_base.py#L235)):*

I'm not sure about the "default logic". I marked those functions to be private just because I want to expose as few public methods as possible, so we have the right to modify them if needed. LG overall however there is some functionality missing.

First, all commit methods should support this, including `Batch.commit()`.
Secondly, we usually call commit on behalf of the user which means they will not be able to pass the options to the commit call themselves. This means we need to provide alternate ways of passing in the options. e.g.
```
database.run_in_transaction(transaction_func, request_options={"priority": "PRIORITY_LOW"})
```
```
for database.batch(request_options={"priority": "PRIORITY_MEDIUM"}) as batch:
    batch.insert(table, columns, data)
```

Additionally, you should update the PR description to give context on the feature and how it should be used. For example, it should mention that all requests are run as `PRIORITY_HIGH` by default and so this feature is to deprioritize tasks.

Hi again,

> So you dropped code coverage completely with this?
> I really appreciate your efforts here, but i don't want to drop code coverage.

I totally agree with you, code coverage tracking and reporting is very important, it must not go away. Thus, I would never dare to remove it ;]. I think there only was a small hiccup at the time when you looked at the outcome here, which has now been fixed.

Other than diligently going through each line of the configuration changes within the review comments, let me explain the situation.

With 1e2d155b and 8bcc6e1b, I tried to bring improved efficiency to both the developer sandbox and CI. However, the changes had to be reverted, because I haven't been able to make it work on CI together with coverage tracking.

I think the reason are some shenanigans related to `tox`, where I didn't have the energy to find out about the root cause.

So, this patch just adds the foundation, and might set the stage for bringing it to CI later. However, it already brings improved efficiency to the developer's sandbox, as you will be able to run the test suite in half of the time as before.

Both of those commands will work as intended, as announced in the original post.

To use it, invoke:

    pytest -vvv --numprocesses=auto

To use it with coverage tracking and reporting, invoke:

    pytest -vvv --numprocesses=auto --cov --cov-report=term-missing

Please note it is a must to use `pytest-cov`, running the process through the `coverage` program does not work here.

With kind regards,
Andreas.

I did a first pass and left some comments. Apart from that, I think it's better to have a `_with_retries` wrapper for all the api methods, for the following reasons:
1. Keep things consistent: in the current state, some api methods do retries while some don't, it's confusing for the developer
2. Keep the option (for the developer) to use the api methods without the retries for all api operations
3. Smaller code changes: easier to reason about them, less error prone

Additionally, we should be very careful to not alter the existing error handling logic while adding the 429 retries.

Thanks @djmitche. This feels like a shadow of the solution, but I imagine it will become clearer when the CDN implementation is added in the next PR.

In Django, middleware are conceptually wrappers around the application, adding layers like an onion that requests and responses go through. They can process and modify requests on the way in, and then can process and modify requests on the way out. Some are primarily request middlewares, for example checking authentication credentials and exiting early. Others are primarily response middleware, for example adding CSP headers. And some act as both request and response handlers, returning `304 Not Modified` responses based on request cache headers, and setting the cache headers on responses. One quirk of this is that middleware are applied in forward order on requests, and reverse order on responses, so they act like layers.

My concept of a CDN middleware would be to get the "raw" URL from the backend response, and then switch out the domain name when processing the response. I'm guessing instead the CDN middleware needs to do this with the request, before the backend can process it? I'm not sure how that logic will work.

But, that's the next PR. This one looks good, and in theory a middleware response path could be added if needed.

With `decode_unicode`, the return value of `iter_lines` are (unicode) strings, without the parameter bytes are returned ([docs](https://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/api/#requests.Response.iter_content)). Since Python 3.6 `json.loads` can luckily handle bytes, but the encoding should be UTF-8, UTF-16 or UTF-32 ([docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/json.html#json.loads)). Can we ensure that the servers' response always has one of these encodings?

Refs #107. The main reason this feature was not implemented seems to be the lack of motivated developers.

Django `bulk_create` has [several documented limitations](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/ref/models/querysets/#bulk-create), but none seem deal breakers.

The opt-in option is probably the best way to achieve backward compatibility and incremental adoption. Also gives an escape hatch for `bulk_create` limitations.

I see no reason to reject the PR, provided the code gets to a mature state and we can get CI on it and good test coverage. I’m actually pretty excited about it.

The idea of collecting "leaf" models and working up the insert chain should work, though we need to be careful of [post generation hooks](https://factoryboy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#post-generation-hooks). Perhaps they should be documented as incompatible with `bulk_create()`, at least as a first step.

I’m not eager to be introducing more DB-specific code in factory_boy, but that seem unavoidable with this feature and can rely on Django attributes to abstract some of the difficulties.

This patch inspects Django models to describe the relationship between objects, where factory_boy is mostly declarative (`SubFactory`, `RelatedFactory` and co). I don’t foresee conflicts arising from that yet, and using Django as the source of truth is probably safe.

My main comment is that we will now have two properties with a very similar content, which seems wasteful. We will never check these for validity.

Instead, my suggestion would be the following:

1. We only store the relative logdir path (let's call it `relative_logdir`)
2. `Trial.logdir` becomes a [cached property](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.cached_property) (use [this](https://pypi.org/project/cached-property/) for python 3.7 compatibility, similar to dataclasses for 3.6)
3. For backwards compatibility, we should extract the relative path when `self.logdir` is set (`@property.setter`), but print a deprecation warning
4. Same for restoring pickled objects (if `state["logdir"]` is set, just call `self.logdir = state.pop("logdir")`

We should add (very short and simple) unit tests to show that these conversions work.

It would also be great to see a test where we move an experiment analysis directory and recover the same information, showing that relative path lookup works.

Let me know if you have bandwidth to add this, otherwise I'm happy to chime in!

I think we need to be sure to remove the paths that were added after calling robot_run.

Imagine that project A runs the robot task from project B as a cross-project task. So this code adds project B's repo_root to sys.path.

Then if there are subsequent tasks from project A that do imports, they might end up resolving modules from project B.

There will still be the potential of leaving modules from project B in sys.modules for any module that was imported during the robot task's execution. (We discussed that, and decided it's unlikely to be a problem since robot libraries tend to be in a path that includes their project name.) But by cleaning up sys.path we can at least avoid causing problems for later imports of modules that weren't imported during the robot task.

Thanks a lot @jrbourbeau for a quick fix of this regression.

I'm eager to help fix the problem. At the same time, I want to understand this bug a bit better in order to avoid creating additional technical debt within zarr-python.

When I first saw @djhoese's issue, I was puzzled by the recommend fix. The example was using local file storage. How is it possible that creating arrays in local files is completely broken in zarr? Surely our test suite would have caught such a regression.

I dug deeper and realized that dask.array.to_zarr is always converting string arguments to FSMap objects (see https://github.com/dask/dask/blob/40bc376acb1f631aa80db17ce4fb779e1a888aee/dask/array/core.py#L3677-L3681) using code like

```python
    if isinstance(url, str):
        mapper = get_mapper(url, **storage_options)
```

FSMap has been effectively deprecated by the introduction of zarr.storage.FSStore (beginning with #546). FSStore has additional performance optimization that are not available with FSMap, and therefore we always prefer to use an FSStore (although an FSMap is still a valid store, as it is a valid MutableMapping).

To address this inconsistency, #1304 recently implemented a change to automatically promote and FSMap to an FSStore (thanks @ravwojdyla! 🙌 ). That change is what revealed this latent bug. Before, the FSMap was initialized with whatever mode the user chose (or the default from fsspec, presumably  `mode='w'`). Now we override this with the default keyword argument from `normalize_store_arg` which is indeed `mode='r'`:

https://github.com/zarr-developers/zarr-python/blob/4e633ad9aa434304296900790c4c65e0fa0dfa12/zarr/storage.py#L144-L147

`mode` is not an attribute of an FSMap, but it is an attribute of FSStore. It is used internally within Zarr to implement read-only behavior, e.g.

https://github.com/zarr-developers/zarr-python/blob/4e633ad9aa434304296900790c4c65e0fa0dfa12/zarr/storage.py#L1409-L1411

My concern is that the `mode` argument has a somewhat ambiguous status within Zarr. It is not supported by all stores, but it could be. That inconsistency is what opened the possibility for this bug in the first place.

Having written all that, I think I have convinced myself that I am fine with this fix. 🙃 

The two follow-up actions I would suggest are:
- Do an audit on the status of `mode` in Zarr. All stores could potentially support mode in the same way that FSStore does. However, we also support a different, redundant method of specifying read-only: via the `Array` and `Group` constructors. `DirectoryStore`, for example, doesn't have `mode` at all, but it is still possible to use read-only stores on disk. Check out [`open_array`](https://github.com/zarr-developers/zarr-python/blob/main/zarr/creation.py#L397) for more details.
- Consider refactoring `dask.array.to_zarr`. Now that Zarr implements url parsing internally via fsspec, it should be possible to leave the store creation completely up to Zarr, rather than Dask.

I guess the reason to have an error accumulator like this is that it's common for many errors to occur at the end of an unsuccessful task, and this provides a simple way to deal with them (`err.add(..)`) without worrying about which came first.

It looks like the only thing that consults any error but the first is WorkerShutdown.  Everything else (`resolve`, `occurred`) ignores all but the first.

Maybe it would make sense to refactor this a little to only keep the first error, with a boolean to track whether a graceful shutdown had occurred (maybe a global?).

I think you can simplify your code a lot if you would not duplicate the entire query in you if statements, but instead build up the filter clause based on the if statements.
something like done here as well: https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/apps/catalogue/managers.py#L39

The code would look something like this then:
```
_filter =  models.Q(includes=self) # start with filter clause that always applies
_exclude_filter = models.Q(excludes=self) # also build and exclude
 if self.classes.exists():
    _filter |= Q(product_class__classes=self)
if Product.objects.filter(parent__includes=self).exists():
    _filter |= Q(parent__includes=self)
    _exclude_filter |= Q(parent__excludes=self)

# etc ...

return Product.objects.filter(_filter).exclude(_exclude_filter)
```
Something like that. You can make the code a LOT shorter if you do it like that and perhaps keep it in the single method?

> I was still mostly unconvinced that relying on get_queryset is really the right way to handle authorization, for the reasons around performance that you quoted from the Django docs, in that it tends to mean very expensive N+1 exponential SQL query fan-out.

For our use case, we have to rely on `get_queryset` to perform auth filtering at the DB level, as pulling all the data from the DB to Python to perform the filter would be a substantial performance/memory cost and would not scale. 

We're tackling the N+1 problem with dataloaders instead of `select_related`/`prefetch_related`. 


> Can I suggest that we update the "Global Filtering" graphene-django docs example to be more similar to that, where it doesn't actually change/filter the queryset, but instead just conditionally returns the queryset? I think it would also be worth noting in those docs something like "Warning: if you modify the queryset, such as performing any filtering here, it will result in N+1 queries when this DjangoObjectType is used as a relation from another Django model in graphene."

Updating the documentation is on my to-do for this PR indeed. Also to be noted that the N+1 issue occurs anyway if you use `graphene-django` without `select_related`/`prefetch_related`, so the comment should be more something like "Warning: modifying the queryset in `get_queryset` will cancel out any optimization technique such as `select_related`/`prefetch_related` in the model's manager (link to Django doc)"

I think we should move this whole logic into the trial to avoid strong coupling between the trial runner and the trial. Basically we want to avoid that the trial runner tells the trial how to update it's internal state.

Instead, we should make the information available to the trial so that it can act based on this. For this case, we would want to know what part of the trial failed.

I can think of a few ways to do that:
1. We introduce `TuneRestoreError`, `TuneTrainingError`, `TuneActorError`, and wrap `exc` in it before passing it to `write_error_log`.
2. We pass something like an `ErrorType` enum

I think I prefer option 1, but open to suggestions.

The trial can then decide which counters to increase and how to write the error log. For instance, it would be helpful to log restore errors as `Restore failure # {}` to distinguish this from training errors in the log file.

For a first step to land it until branch cut, we can just distinguish between restore and non-restore errors, if that's easier.

As a minor refactor note, I think we should rename `write_error_log` to `handle_error` and only call it when an error is actually set (i.e. move the `if exc` into the trial runner and remove the `Optional` annotation in the method).

What do you think?

Across the commented spots, besides minor issues and the existing suggestions, I see two major issues to be discussed more broadly:
- `.url` vs `.local_filename` in the presence of the METS server: To me it seems there still has been a confusion here. Whether I use a METS server should not relate at all to whether a referenced file is a URL or local path (i.e. whether files are downloaded from their original/source location). So the METS server should usually pass on both of these attributes. And if indeed we want to use the METS server itself to also handle files remotely (see next point), as is currently implemented with `add_file(content=...)`, then IIUC that **must not** be referenced remotely (as `url`) but only locally (as `local_filename`).
- `content` kwarg and "remote workspaces": I don't see the point of squeezing the file content through the METS server at all. The METS server must be where the METS file is. (And so must the processor for various reasons.) We have the `content` kwarg of `Workspace.add_file` as a convenience for serialization to METS and to filesystem in one shot. If that METS is behind a server, you might argue, the fs action must coincide exactly with the METS action. But temporary inconsistency is unavoidable anyway – and for two reasons: a) because there would be no guarantee when the server synchronises the METS to the fs, and b) because  `add_file` can be used with existing files (`content=None`). The task of the METS server is to synchronise METS actions, not fs actions. So let's keep the `content` kwarg out of the client-server logic.

> I was wondering whether we should decouple this further, so that a processor can request a file and download it via the METS server, store it in a local scratch directory (or just keep in memory if feasible), do the processing and upload the file again. That way there would be no need to have a processor-local full workspace at all.

The current METS server only has facilities for _output_ (`add_file(content=...)`), but not for _input_ (`download_file(...)`). Assuming that we can rewrite / guarantee all processors to do all I/O via the (extended) METS server, then processors would not need to run in the workspace directory, yes. And the client side could keep files in memory by some criterion (albeit not for bashlib processors of course, and with significant extra effort to avoid leaks and invalidity), yes. 

But what would we need that for? Processors need fast I/O. Having the data near the CPU (in memory or RAM disks or other fast storage) is one side, the other is latency of the transfer and whether that transfer contributes to reduced throughput because it cannot be adequately multiplexed with processing. So we must look at the overall architecture (esp. of parallelisation options).

For the moment we already have `Workspace.download_file` (which is used almost 100% for input retrieval in the current processor codebase). If the file is a URL, this will silently (without modifying the METS) download it to a reproducible temporary location. Subsequent calls will reuse the same file (i.e. avoid redownload). So if we avoid paying the cost of the initial transfer, and just keep URLs for all images of the workspace, we can currently get a system without much initial latency (processing will start after downloading the first page's input file). As soon as we move towards page-parallel processing, we will also have  good utilisation of CPU resources (where some workers process while others transfer; the number of jobs should be a little higher than the number of CPU cores available).

The alternative would be an explicit transfer model: prior download to a fast location, explicit queueing of transfer jobs intertwined by processing jobs. But we are still far away from such an architecture IMO. (And I don't quite see the METS I/O server fit into that paradigm anyway.)

> Maybe we should first try to identify a spot where the METS Server fits the best in this [architecture](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OCR-D/spec/c09bf8f6792af7d8a920a58700d784d02d146643/images/web-api-distributed-queue.jpg). I think the METS Server fits the best between the Processing Servers (workers) and the Network File System. It acts as a proxy when a path to the image has to be provided or an image to be downloaded. So the Mets server will not have to store workspace images locally. Since I did not put much thought into this idea yet, maybe there are things I have not considered.

I disagree. In the original conception, the METS server had nothing to do with I/O of files other than METS. For the moment, the `content` handling is partial (only output) and not necessary. I believe it is an internal detail of the Processing Servers (→Workers) which allows a multitude of threads/processes working on the same workspace. Of course, we could manage central METS servers from the Processing Broker (→Processing Server), too. 

BTW, with the page-parallel processor API, if we factor METS actions out of `process_page` (making it a function returning OcrdFile instead of a procedure returning boolean), we could even write the new central loop `process_workspace` using multiprocessing with METS synchronisation without METS server.

small change

Apart from the ones I fixed, there are more operators that use the `.view(-1, ...)` idiom. 

- https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/4c073b09521604e410bac8a0b0fe0f2680724e0d/torchvision/prototype/transforms/functional/_geometry.py#L227
- https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/4c073b09521604e410bac8a0b0fe0f2680724e0d/torchvision/prototype/transforms/functional/_geometry.py#L448

I've xfailed some tests for them. Plus, there are more bounding box ops that do this. But we don't need to worry about them, because `.view(-1, ...)` only fails if there is a `0` in the `...` part. For bounding boxes we only do 

```py
>>> t = torch.empty(0, 4)
>>> t.view(-1, 4)
tensor([], size=(0, 4))
```

The ambiguity only arises for segmentation masks, which use the channel dimension as number of objects and that can be `0`.

```py
>>> t = torch.empty(4, 0, 16, 16)
>>> t.view(-1, 0, 16, 16)
RuntimeError: cannot reshape tensor of 0 elements into shape [-1, 0, 16, 16] because the unspecified dimension size -1 can be any value and is ambiguous
```

That might be a good reason to have something in the kernel other than just calling the image kernel. But we can discuss this later.

> The only issue I see with this (softmax) converter is that it requires at least the rank of the input tensor. (This can be improved if we omit the optimization at the beginning for a special case.) LGTM overall (should wait for others to validate the API), but just to make sure I understand the limitations of this:

1. When working with a future representing the return object of a task, future cancellation will not cancel the corresponding task, and we'd need a tighter concurrent.futures integration (e.g. Ray implementing a concurrent.futures executor) in order to support that, right?
2. In the same vein, `future.running()` will always be `False`, even if the task that produces the future is currently running.

Are there any concerns with only supporting the semantics of a subset of the `concurrent.futures.Future` API? My first impression is that this is fine for now, and that this shouldn't be blocked by a proper `concurrent.futures.Executor` implementation.

Hey @ABD-01 and thanks for the continuing effort. I have some minor comments inline. Apart from them, two more things:

1. Please also add the new datasets to our documentation as shortly outlined in https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/4255#pullrequestreview-732488800
2. I have not yet reviewed the `_get_people` and `_get_pairs` methods, as they really depend on the data format, which I don't know yet. However, this will be solved by the next point:

> The tests are remaining so I'll add them, could you please guide me on how to proceed with that?

Sure! You need to create two test cases, `LFWPeopleTestCase` and `LFWPairsTestCase`, in  [`tess_datasets.py`](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/main/test/test_datasets.py) that inherit from 

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/7947fc8fb38b1d3a2aca03f22a2e6a3caa63f2a0/test/datasets_utils.py#L180

Please read the docstring to get a better understanding what needs to be done. 

The absolute minimum that you need to provide, is an implementation of the `inject_fake_data` method. It gets passed a `tmp_dir` that needs to contain all files in the correct structure that would be present in `root` if I would instantiate the dataset with `LFWPeople(root, download=True)`. If the content changes depending on other keyword arguments, you can query them from the passed `config` dict, i.e. `config["train"]`. If you overwrite the class attribute `ADDITIONAL_CONFIGS` for example with 

```python
ADDITIONAL_CONFIGS = datasets_utils.combinations_grid(
    train=(True, False), image_set=("original", "funneled", "deepfunneled"),
)
```

you will get automate testing for all combinations. 

Of course the files provided by `inject_fake_data` musn't be the original files, but rather mock ups of them, i.e. "fake data". For example, you will want to create a `peopleDevTrain.txt` file, that only contains a few lines. For the accompanying images, you can use convenience utilities such as 

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/7947fc8fb38b1d3a2aca03f22a2e6a3caa63f2a0/test/datasets_utils.py#L666

or 

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/7947fc8fb38b1d3a2aca03f22a2e6a3caa63f2a0/test/datasets_utils.py#L702

For the LFW dataset, I propose you add a `_LFWTestCase` that handles the injection of fake data so that the actual test cases only need to handle their special stuff.

---

If all of the above is "too much", we can also do it step by step. Implement something and if you hit a roadblock, push the commit and ping me so I can take a look at it and guide you more precisely.
